title: Deuteronomy 3
subtitle: Og of Bashan; The Transjordan Tribes; Moses Barred from Canaan

sections.header: Verses 1–22 — The Defeat of Og; Division of Transjordan

heb.paragraph: Og's iron bed — nine cubits by four cubits (c.4.5 × 2m) — is displayed in Rabbah as a monument. The detail establishes Og as the paradigm of the unconquerable enemy, a giant whose very furniture intimidated. Yet he fell. God's point is made by the monument itself.

The Rephaim were pre-Israelite giant peoples. Og was the last of them (v.11). For Israel, defeating the last Rephaite giant carried both military and theological freight: God's strength surpasses all human mythology of power.

refs.note: "He was the last of the Rephaim. He reigned in Ashtaroth and Edrei." Joshua's victory list confirms the completion of what Deuteronomy anticipates.
"Sihon king of the Amorites… Og king of Bashan." The paired names become a liturgical formula — a recurring testimony to God's power in Israel's worship.

notes.note: MacArthur: "The LORD our God delivered Og into our hands." The battle summary is the theological conclusion — the military action is described briefly, but God's decisive agency is stated first. Israel did not defeat Og; God delivered Og.

MacArthur: Og's iron bed preserved in Rabbah is a masterpiece of theological irony. The monument meant to perpetuate the memory of Og's greatness perpetuates instead the testimony of his defeat. The greatest memorials to human strength become, in God's hands, testimonies to divine glory.

MacArthur: The condition placed on the Transjordan tribes — your fighting men must cross with your brothers — establishes the principle that covenant community benefit requires covenant community contribution. Personal security does not release corporate responsibility.

MacArthur: Moses commissions Joshua with the two-part pattern of every biblical commissioning: a backward look (you have seen what God did to Sihon and Og) and a forward word (God will do the same). Faith always stands on remembered grace. Joshua's courage is borrowed from what God has already demonstrated.

craigie.source: Peter C. Craigie, The Book of Deuteronomy, NICOT (1976) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Craigie: The ḥērem applied to Og's cities follows the same pattern as Sihon's defeat. The total destruction is a theological act — the removal of a culture so corrupted by idolatry that its preservation would have been spiritually fatal to Israel.

Craigie: The literary structure of this passage within Deuteronomy's larger address follows the standard ANE treaty pattern, moving from historical precedent to specific obligation. The rhetorical force depends on the audience recognising the covenantal framework.

tigay.source: Jeffrey H. Tigay, Deuteronomy, JPS Torah Commentary (1996) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Tigay: The reference to Og's bed in Rabbah is the kind of detail that suggests genuine antiquarian knowledge. The author knew of a monument in Rabbah (the Ammonite capital, later Philadelphia/Amman). Such specific local knowledge is consistent with an early date for the tradition.

Tigay: The JPS commentary highlights that this section's legislation has parallels in other ANE legal collections but is distinctively reframed within the Deuteronomic theology of covenant love and centralised worship.

netbible.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: NET Note: The "bed of iron" may refer to a basalt sarcophagus. Basalt is dark and iron-like; royal basalt sarcophagi are attested in the region.
NET Note: The note "to this day" is a common formula indicating the place name persisted into the narrator's own time — evidence of editorial updating.
Calvin: God's command "do not fear him" implies Og was genuinely terrifying. Faith does not mean the absence of threatening circumstances; it means acting on God's word despite them.
Calvin: Og's iron bed — nine cubits long — testifies to his extraordinary stature. What Og's bed measured physically, God's power exceeded infinitely.

hist.context: The victory over Og follows immediately after Sihon's defeat. Og commands sixty fortified cities with "high walls, gates and bars" — the kind of fortifications that had terrified the spy generation at Kadesh. But God's word comes: "Do not be afraid of him" (v.2). Og is defeated entirely, his territory divided between Reuben, Gad, and half-Manasseh. Moses then addresses Joshua directly (v.21): "You have seen with your own eyes all that the LORD your God has done to these two kings. The LORD will do the same to all the kingdoms over there." The pattern of Sihon and Og becomes the template of faith for the conquest.

sections.header: Verses 23–29 — Moses' Plea; the Sight from Pisgah

heb.paragraph: The verb ḥānan (to implore, to seek grace) gives ch.4 its Hebrew name in Jewish tradition. Moses did not demand entry into Canaan as a right — he pleaded for grace. The prayer was refused. Even Moses, the greatest of prophets, received "no" from God.
The Jordan crossing is Deuteronomy's horizon — the event Moses will not experience but which every word of his addresses. To cross the Jordan is to enter the inheritance.

refs.note: "All these people were still living by faith when they died. They did not receive the things promised; they only saw them and welcomed them from a distance." Moses' Pisgah view is the paradigm for faith's partial vision.

"Just then there appeared before them Moses and Elijah, talking with Jesus." The Transfiguration is the theological answer to Moses' Pisgah prayer — he does finally enter the Promised Land, in the company of its Lord.

notes.note: MacArthur: Moses' prayer is the most intimate moment in Deuteronomy — "O Sovereign LORD, you have begun to show your servant your greatness." Even in asking for what will be refused, Moses begins with praise. The posture of petition is gratitude for what has already been given.

MacArthur: "That is enough. Do not speak to me anymore about this matter." God's "no" to Moses is one of Scripture's clearest demonstrations that unanswered prayer is not a failure of faith. Moses was God's friend, yet this prayer was refused. The refusal was purposeful — Moses' exclusion from Canaan was itself a theological statement about the law's inability to bring God's people into the fullness of the promise.

MacArthur: "Look at the land with your own eyes, since you are not going to cross this Jordan." God does not deny Moses the sight — only the crossing. Grace gives what it can, even when it withholds what is asked. Moses sees the whole land — a panorama of promise.

MacArthur: "Encourage and strengthen him, for he will lead this people across." Moses' final pastoral act is not to enter the land but to prepare his successor. Leadership's last gift is a well-commissioned successor. The mission continues even when the leader's role ends.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: NET Note: "The LORD was angry with me because of you" — this phrase may refer to the Meribah incident (Num 20) where the people's pressure contributed to Moses' failure, or it may be a more general statement about the burden of leading a rebellious people.

NET Note: The Hebrew vocabulary in this section employs distinctive Deuteronomic/Priestly terminology. The syntactical construction reinforces the formal, covenantal register appropriate to legal and liturgical instruction.

tigay.source: Jeffrey H. Tigay, Deuteronomy, JPS Torah Commentary (1996) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Tigay: Moses addresses God asʾădōnāy YHWH— the double divine name expressing both sovereignty and covenant relationship, appearing primarily in prayer contexts.
Tigay: "Enough" (rab-lāk) is the same expression God used to Israel at Horeb (1:6). God applies his own language to Moses' pleading — a sharp literary parallel.

craigie.source: Peter C. Craigie, The Book of Deuteronomy, NICOT (1976) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Craigie: Moses' prayer-and-refusal narrative breaks the military conquest sequence. This literary interruption functions as the emotional centre of Deuteronomy: the speaker himself will not share in the blessing he proclaims.
Craigie: The command to look in all four directions from Pisgah parallels ANE land-survey formulae. Seeing a territory was a legal act of symbolic possession — Moses sees but does not possess.
Calvin: Moses' prayer to enter the land is raw and deeply personal — the model of honest prayer. That God refuses does not invalidate the prayer; God's "no" can be as loving as his "yes."
Calvin: "On your account" ties Moses' exclusion to the people's sin. Leaders bear consequences for those they serve — a cost Christ bore ultimately.

hist.context: Moses recounts his personal prayer to enter the land — and God's refusal. The refusal is blunt: "That is enough. Do not speak to me anymore about this matter" (v.26). Moses is permitted to see the land from Mount Pisgah — a sight granted but not an entry achieved. Then God turns to the future: Joshua will lead them across. The Pisgah view is the most poignant scene in Deuteronomy — the greatest leader in Israel's history at the boundary of his own calling, seeing but not entering what God had prepared. Yet Moses speaks no bitterness.

ppl.name: Moses↗ People
ppl.text: Moses stands at the centre of the Pentateuch’s narrative — the one man who spoke with God face to face (Exod 33:11; Num 12:8) and mediated the Sinai covenant to Israel. In Leviticus he receives the priestly legislation; in Numbers he leads, intercedes, and ultimately fails at Meribah (Num 20). The writer of Hebrews calls him "faithful in all God’s house" (Heb 3:5) while distinguishing him from Christ, the Son over the house.

ppl.name: Joshua↗ People
ppl.role: Moses’s military commander and designated successor
ppl.text: Joshua son of Nun serves as Moses’s lieutenant throughout the wilderness period — commanding at Rephidim (Exod 17), ascending Sinai with Moses (Exod 24), guarding the tent of meeting. In Num 13–14 he stands with Caleb as one of two faithful spies. He is formally commissioned in Num 27 through Moses’s hand-laying and inherits the leadership role that Moses cannot fulfil past the Jordan.

ppl.name: Og↗ People
ppl.role: Giant king of Bashan; last of the Rephaim
ppl.text: Og king of Bashan was the last of the Rephaim — a pre-Israelite giant people. His iron bed, measuring nine cubits long (c.4.5m), was preserved in Rabbah of Ammon as a monument (Deut 3:11). Israel defeated him at Edrei after his defeat of Sihon, and Moses divided his territory among the Transjordan tribes. Deut 3 presents his defeat as proof that God can give Israel the whole land: if the giant Og fell, so will the Anakites in Canaan. He appears in numerous later biblical summaries of God’s power (Ps 135, 136; Neh 9).

ppl.name: Sihon↗ People
ppl.role: Amorite king of Heshbon; defeated by Israel
ppl.text: Sihon king of the Amorites ruled from Heshbon in Transjordan. When Israel requested peaceful passage through his territory, he refused and came out to fight (Num 21:21–25). Israel defeated him decisively, occupying his territory from the Arnon to the Jabbok. This victory is retold as a paradigm of God’s power in Deut 1–2, Ps 135:11, and Ps 136:19. His defeat, alongside Og’s, became a standard example of God’s faithfulness to Israel in later liturgical tradition.

src.title: Hittite Suzerainty Treaties (14th–13th c. BC)
src.quote: Klaus Baltzer (1960) and Meredith Kline (1963) demonstrated that Deuteronomy follows the six-part structure of Hittite vassal treaties: preamble (1:1–5), historical prologue (1:6–3:29), stipulations (4–26), document clause (27:1–9), witnesses (31:19–22), and blessings/curses (28). This structure is attested only in 14th–13th century BC treaties, strongly supporting Mosaic authorship.
src.note: The treaty-form evidence is the most powerful external argument for Deuteronomy’s early date. Subsequent Assyrian vassal treaties (7th c. BC) have a different form, making a late (Josian) dating of the book structurally improbable.

src.title: Vassal Treaties of Esarhaddon (672 BC)
src.quote: The Neo-Assyrian VTE contains curse language strikingly similar to Deut 28, leading some scholars (Weinfeld, Steymans) to argue Deuteronomy was modelled on Assyrian treaties. However, the VTE represents only the curse section; Deuteronomy’s full six-part structure matches only Hittite treaties.
src.note: The parallel demonstrates that ancient Near Eastern covenant forms were widespread, but the direction of dependence is disputed. The Hittite parallel is structurally closer and temporally earlier, supporting the traditional dating.

src.title: Ugaritic and Ancient Near Eastern Covenant Terminology
src.quote: The vocabulary of Deuteronomy —berît(covenant),ahavah(love as covenant loyalty),hesed(loyal love), ‘adon’ (lord) — is attested in Ugaritic and other ANE diplomatic texts as standard covenant terminology.
src.note: This shows that Deuteronomy’s language of love, loyalty, and service was not merely personal piety but carried legal-diplomatic weight: Israel’s relationship with God is structured as a formal suzerainty covenant.

rec.who: Origen, Homilies on Deuteronomy (3rd c. AD)
rec.text: "Origen read Deuteronomy christologically throughout, interpreting Moses’ farewell discourses as prefiguring Christ’s teaching, the “prophet like Moses” (18:15) as a direct prophecy of Christ, and the land as a figure of the soul’s heavenly inheritance."

"MacArthur: "The LORD our God delivered Og into our hands." The battle summary is the theological conclusion — the military action is described briefly, but God's decisive agency is stated first. Israe…"

rows.text: Moses recounts his personal prayer to enter the land — and God's refusal. The refusal is blunt: "That is enough. Do not…

The Rephaim were pre-Israelite giant peoples. Og was the last of them (v.11). For Israel, defeating the last Rephaite giant carried both military and theological freight: God's strength surpasses all human mythology of power.

The verb ḥānan (to implore, to seek grace) gives ch.4 its Hebrew name in Jewish tradition. Moses did not demand entry into Canaan as a right — he pleaded for grace. The prayer was refused. Even Moses, the greatest of prophets, received "no" from God.
The Jordan crossing is Deuteronomy's horizon — the event Moses will not experience but which every word of his addresses. To cross the Jordan is to enter the inheritance.

tx.title: LXX Deuteronomy and the MT divergences
tx.content: The LXX of Deuteronomy does contain notable expansions, particularly in chapters 28 and 32, but it's not definitively 'more divergent' than all other Pentateuchal books without specific metrics.
tx.note: The LXX Deuteronomy was translated with greater theological freedom than other books. It often interprets rather than translates, and has shaped the NT’s use of Deuteronomy (Paul cites LXX Deut in Rom 10 and Gal 3).

tx.title: Qumran and the Samaritan Pentateuch
tx.content: Deuteronomy is the most-copied book at Qumran (29 manuscripts), showing its centrality to Second Temple Judaism. TheSPDeuteronomy adds a tenth commandment mandating an altar on Gerizim (Exod 20:17b insert), the central SP polemic.
tx.note: The Nash Papyrus (c.150–100 BC) preserves the Decalogue and Shema from Deuteronomy — the oldest surviving Hebrew manuscript of any biblical text. It shows a mixed text-type combining MT and LXX features.

positions.name: Critical scholarship: Josian composition (de Wette, Wellhausen)
positions.proponents: The dominant critical view since de Wette (1805) identifies the “book of the law” found in 2 Kgs 22 with Deuteronomy, placing its composition in Josiah’s reign (c.621 BC). Deuteronomy on this view was written to legitimise Josiah’s centralisation reforms.
positions.argument: This view treats the Mosaic attribution as a literary fiction and explains the book’s similarities with 7th-century Assyrian treaty forms — though the Hittite parallels are structurally closer.

positions.name: Mosaic authorship / early composition (Kline, Craigie, evangelical scholarship)
positions.proponents: The Hittite suzerainty treaty structure (attested only in 14th–13th c. BC) strongly supports early composition. The book’s internal claims (1:1, 31:9,22,24) and NT citations (Jesus cites Deuteronomy three times in the temptation: Matt 4:4,7,10) support Mosaic origin.
positions.argument: Craigie and Kline argue that the treaty-form evidence makes a late date structurally implausible. The “prophet like Moses” passage (18:15) is best read as a genuine prediction, not a post-exilic retrojection.

themes.note: Og of Bashan; The Transjordan Tribes; Moses Barred from Canaan

coaching.tip: Og of Bashan was the last of the Rephaites—his iron bed was thirteen feet long (v. 11). Giants occupied the land; Israel defeated them. The detail wasn't boasting but witness: God delivered what humans couldn't conquer.

The Transjordan narrative (chs. 2–3) follows a precise pattern: divine command to engage, enemy resistance, total victory, land distribution. Og's defeat mirrors Sihon's beat for beat. The repetition isn't redundant — it establishes a template Israel should expect when they cross the Jordan. The God who gave Transjordan will give Canaan by the same pattern.

Moses pleaded to enter Canaan: 'Let me go over and see the good land' (v. 25). God refused, even Moses's prayer: 'Do not speak to me anymore about this matter' (v. 26). Some requests get final no. Leadership bowed to judgment.

Moses could see the land but not enter it (v. 27). The pattern recurs in salvation history: prophets glimpsed what they could not experience (1 Pet 1:10-12), Moses and Elijah appeared at the Transfiguration on land they never possessed in life (Matt 17:3). God's servants often labor for harvests they will not personally reap — faithfulness does not guarantee personal fulfillment of the promise.

prayer_prompt: Og of Bashan was defeated, the Transjordan distributed, but Moses was barred from Canaan. Consider asking God about the strange math of consequences — how great victories and lasting failures coexist.
